findings.claim: DeepResearch Bench now publishes TWO separate leaderboards with different judges, and the GPT-5.5 one (the one that matches our evaluator) is a small, freshly-rescored table — not the table ADORE's 52.65 came from.
findings.detail: The HF space muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard ships create_leaderboard.py with two tabs: 'Leaderboard (GPT-5.5 Eval)' (create_leaderboard_tab_gpt55, data source data_gpt55/, RACE judge = GPT-5.5, FACT = GPT-5.4-mini) and 'Leaderboard (Gemini-2.5 Eval)' (create_leaderboard_tab, data source data/, RACE judge = gemini-2.5-pro, FACT = gemini-2.5-flash). The Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench README states the switch happened 2026-05-11 (prompted by Google's announced 2026-06-17 deprecation of Gemini-2.5-Pro), dual submissions accepted until 2026-05-31, full migration to GPT-5.5 on 2026-06-01. GPT-5.5 was chosen because it 'wins on Overall, PAR, and FAS' with 71.82% agreement vs 68.78% human inter-annotator baseline.

findings.claim: The CURRENT GPT-5.5-judged leaderboard (our evaluator) has only 10 entries; SOTA is cellcog-max at 55.78, and ADORE is not on it at all.
findings.detail: Verbatim data_gpt55/leaderboard.csv (model, overall, comprehensiveness, insight, instruction_following, readability): cellcog-max 55.78 / 56.34 / 57.08 / 55.30 / 51.94; WhaleCloud-DocChain_0612 54.78 / 55.14 / 55.33 / 54.85 / 52.48; bodhi 54.07 / 54.15 / 54.60 / 54.41 / 51.87; lunon_full100_FINAL 53.51 / 53.42 / 54.83 / 53.41 / 50.48; dalpha-deepresearch 53.10 / 52.58 / 52.94 / 53.87 / 53.20; sourcery 51.17 / 50.53 / 52.22 / 51.06 / 49.68; gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch 49.98 / 50.01 / 49.92 / 50.22 / 49.58; openai-deepresearch 47.84 / 48.05 / 46.69 / 49.29 / 47.62; perplexity-Research 43.05; grok-deeper-search 41.22. Six systems are above 0.50. FACT columns are all '-' (not yet re-run under GPT-5.4-mini).

findings.claim: ADORE's 52.65 was measured on the LEGACY Gemini-2.5-Pro judge, in a January 2026 paper, and its '#1 on the leaderboard' claim is now ~18 months of leaderboard-churn stale. Do NOT treat 52.65 as SOTA-on-our-evaluator.
findings.detail: arXiv 2601.18267 ('Orchestrating Specialized Agents for Trustworthy Enterprise RAG', PDF footer 'Generated on Mon Jan 26 08:48:29 2026') Table 2 lists: 1 ADORE 52.65/52.22/54.37/51.11/52.18; 2 Tavily Research 52.44/52.84/53.59/51.92/49.21; 3 ThinkDepthAI 52.43/52.02/53.88/52.04/50.12; 4 CellCog 51.94; 5 Salesforce Air 50.65/50.00/51.09/50.77/50.32; 6 LangChain(GPT-5) 50.60; 7 Gemini 2.5 Pro 49.71; 8 LangChain(Tavily) 49.33; 9 OpenAI-DR 46.45; 10 Claude Research 45.00. Every one of those baseline rows is a byte-for-byte match to the CURRENT Gemini-2.5-Pro-judged CSV (tavily-research 52.44/52.84/53.59/51.92/49.21; thinkdepthai 52.43; salesforce-air 50.65; gensee-search-gpt-5 50.60; gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch 49.71; openai-deepresearch 46.45). That proves ADORE scored against the Gemini-2.5-Pro leaderboard, months before GPT-5.5 existed as the judge. The paper never names its judge model — it just cites the bench.

findings.claim: On the legacy Gemini-2.5-Pro leaderboard as it stands TODAY, 52.65 would rank roughly 22nd of 45. Top is qianfan_deepresearch_0430 at 58.03.
findings.detail: Verbatim head of data/leaderboard.csv (Gemini-2.5 tab): qianfan_deepresearch_0430 58.03 / 59.48 / 61.48 / 53.87 / 54.34; ZTE-Nebula-DeepResearch-V20260519 57.27; Link 57.08; zhipu_deep_research 57.06; xiaoyi 57.00; WhaleCloud-DocChain 56.81; cellcog-max 56.67 / 57.40 / 60.01 / 53.25 / 53.21; 1688AILab-DeepResearch-0428 56.53; octen 56.31; grep-v5 56.23; nvidia-aiq-nemotron-gpt52 55.95; ... TrajectoryKit 54.92; onyx 54.54; ... tavily-research 52.44 (rank ~23). So ADORE's 52.65 now sits just above Tavily, far below the pack. ADORE appears on neither current CSV.

findings.claim: RACE absolute numbers are NOT portable across judge models or even across judge runs — the same fixed report (gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch) scores 49.71, 49.98, and 52.54 depending on who ran the judge.
findings.detail: 49.71 on the official Gemini-2.5-Pro leaderboard; 49.98 on the official GPT-5.5 leaderboard; 52.54 when the DualGraph authors re-ran the *same downloaded reports* through their own judge instance ('To align the LLM judge versions, we obtain the reports of proprietary systems from DeepResearch Bench and re-evaluate them using the same judge model'). Any paper number you compare against must be checked for which judge and which run produced it. Practical consequence: our GPT-5.5 k=5 pinned 0.4382 is only comparable to the data_gpt55 column, i.e. gap-to-SOTA is 55.78 - 43.82 = 11.96 pts, and gap-to-human is 6.18 pts — bigger than the 8.8 we assumed against 52.65.

findings.claim: Under the GPT-5.5 judge, READABILITY is the dimension where even SOTA barely beats the human reference, while INSIGHT and COMPREHENSIVENESS are where the headroom is. The Gemini judge inflated insight; GPT-5.5 compresses it.
findings.detail: GPT-5.5 tab, top-6 systems: readability spans 49.68-53.20 (mean ~51.6) while insight spans 52.22-57.08 and comprehensiveness 50.53-56.34. Same systems on the Gemini tab show insight far higher relative to readability (cellcog-max: insight 60.01 vs readability 53.21 under Gemini; insight 57.08 vs readability 51.94 under GPT-5.5). So under GPT-5.5 the judge is stingier on insight and roughly flat on readability — nobody scores >53.2 readability. Our 37.74 readability is ~14 pts below the SOTA band and ~12 below the human reference: that is the single largest per-dimension deficit we have, even though its weight is only 0.14 on task 72.

findings.claim: The mechanism that moves INSIGHT specifically is a cross-source knowledge graph built during retrieval, kept SEPARATE from the outline/section plan — and it moves insight and comprehensiveness while leaving instruction-following and readability flat.
findings.detail: DualGraph (arXiv 2602.13830, ICML-format, Microsoft; 53.08 RACE with GPT-5 backbone) keeps two graphs: an Outline Graph (section structure) and a Knowledge Graph (entities + relations harvested across sources). Ablation, GPT-5 backbone: adding the KG raises Comprehensiveness 51.69 -> 53.78 (+2.09) and Insight 51.79 -> 54.30 (+2.51), 'while instruction-following or readability change modestly, suggesting KG mainly helps surface missing aspects and deepen analysis rather than formatting.' It also lifts effective citations 66.06 -> 79.65 (+13.59) while citation accuracy drops 61.15 -> 57.55. This is direct evidence for our hypothesis that span-grounded, single-source sentences structurally cap insight: the fix is an explicit cross-source relation store that the writer is allowed to synthesize from, not more sections.

findings.claim: Onyx is an open-source DRB submission (54.54 on the Gemini tab) that publishes its actual generated reports — a directly imitable structure corpus.
findings.detail: github.com/onyx-dot-app/onyx_deep_research_bench: reports live in report_logs/. Their stated setup: GPT-5.2 backbone, Serper for search, Firecrawl for crawling, 30-minute cap per question, max 2 minutes between user-facing outputs, and — notably — 'Outputs were tuned for human usability and conciseness - the reports are typically around 10,000 tokens'. The repo README claims 54.92/rank-1 (self-reported); the current Gemini CSV lists onyx at 54.54 (TrajectoryKit holds 54.92). Worth pulling report_logs to measure their paragraph/H3/table/bullet distribution against the human reference profile we already computed.

findings.claim: A successor benchmark, 'DeepResearch Bench II', already exists and drops FACT for InfoRecall plus fully-verifiable rubrics that do not rely on the judge's internal knowledge.
findings.detail: Cited in DualGraph as Li et al., 2026a: DRB-II 'replaces the FACT metric with InfoRecall for evaluating information retrieval capability' and 'adopts fine-grained, fully verifiable rubrics that do not rely on the judge model's internal domain knowledge'. It scores InfoRecall / Analysis / Presentation (DualGraph: Total 41.48, InfoRecall 34.21, Analysis 52.45). If we are optimizing hard against RACE's ArticleCleaner quirk (citations stripped), note the successor bench re-introduces retrieval scoring — so citation-invisibility is a RACE-v1-only exploit.

findings.claim: Reported open-source/paper numbers above 53 exist (VeriTrace ~55.77, FS-Researcher 53.94) but I could not verify their judge model from primary text — treat as non-comparable until checked.
findings.detail: VeriTrace (arXiv 2605.26081, submitted 2026-05-25, right at the judge switch) abstract only says it 'improves over the strongest matched baseline by 4.22 pp on DRB Insight (1.49 pp Overall)' with Qwen3.5-27B and claims 'the strongest reproducible open-source result on DRB' with Config-DeepSeek; the 55.77 figure came from a search snippet, not the abstract, and the abstract names no judge. FS-Researcher 53.94 (Claude-Sonnet-4.5, arXiv 2602.01566) likewise unverified. Both predate or straddle the GPT-5.5 migration, so they are most likely Gemini-2.5-Pro-judged.

findings.claim: RACE's 50.0 = human reference calibration is confirmed in the primary literature, and beating it is normal now: 6/10 systems on the GPT-5.5 tab and ~25/45 on the Gemini tab are above 50.
findings.detail: ADORE paper states explicitly: 'Given that the benchmark is calibrated such that a score of 50.0 represents the human-written reference, ADORE demonstrates a 14.11% improvement in quality over human baselines' (for its internal 64.11 enterprise run). The public-bench implication for us: beating the human reference on DRB is table stakes for a 2026 submission, not a stretch goal — the frontier is +5.8 over the human reference (55.78 under GPT-5.5), and we are currently -6.2 under it.

findings.claim: ADORE is NOT SOTA on DeepResearch Bench, and 52.65 is not the number to beat. ADORE does not appear on EITHER official leaderboard (legacy Gemini-2.5-Pro or current GPT-5.5). Its '#1' claim was true only against a ~Jan-2026 leaderboard snapshot that is now badly stale.
findings.detail: I pulled the leaderboard's actual source-of-truth CSVs from the HF Space repo (not the rendered page). ADORE's paper Table 2 lists its competitors as Tavily 52.44, ThinkDepthAI 52.43, CellCog 51.94, Salesforce Air 50.65, LangChain(GPT-5) 50.60 — those rows match the LEGACY leaderboard exactly (tavily-research 52.44, thinkdepthai 52.43, salesforce-air 50.65, gensee-search-gpt-5 50.60), confirming ADORE benchmarked against a legacy-judge snapshot. But on the CURRENT legacy board, 21 systems now sit ABOVE 52.65 (qianfan 58.03, ZTE-Nebula 57.27, Link 57.08, zhipu 57.06, xiaoyi 57.00, WhaleCloud 56.81, cellcog-max 56.67, 1688AILab 56.53, octen 56.31, grep-v5 56.23, nvidia-aiq 55.95, ... RecallRadar 53.19). Note CellCog was 51.94 in ADORE's snapshot and is 56.67 today — the board moved ~4-5 pts under ADORE. ADORE would rank ~#22 today. Do not plan against 0.5265 as 'SOTA'; this is exactly the 48.88 mistake repeating.

findings.claim: The real SOTA under POLARIS's own judge (GPT-5.5) is cellcog-max at 55.78, not 52.65. POLARIS's true gap is ~0.120, not 0.088.
findings.detail: The benchmark switched its official RACE evaluator from Gemini-2.5-Pro to GPT-5.5 on 11 May 2026 (Gemini-2.5-Pro deprecation), and maintains a SEPARATE data_gpt55/leaderboard.csv. Full GPT-5.5 board: cellcog-max 55.78 (comp 56.34 / insight 57.08 / IF 55.30 / read 51.94), WhaleCloud-DocChain 54.78, bodhi 54.07, lunon 53.51, dalpha 53.10, sourcery 51.17, gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch 49.98, openai-deepresearch 47.84, perplexity 43.05, grok-deeper-search 41.22. Since POLARIS is measured with the gpt-5.5 evaluator, this is the ONLY directly comparable board. Cross-judge scores are NOT comparable and the mapping is nonlinear (compresses the top, lifts the bottom): cellcog 56.67→55.78, gemini-DR 49.71→49.98, openai-DR 46.45→47.84, perplexity 40.46→43.05, grok 38.22→41.22. Target 55.78.

findings.claim: The single highest-value, directly-actionable result in the literature: WebWeaver's ablation shows that switching from MONOLITHIC composition to HIERARCHICAL section-by-section writing — with retrieval held constant — moves insight 42.72 → 50.02 (+7.3) and readability 43.82 → 49.79 (+6.0). This is precisely POLARIS's failure mode and it is a writer-side fix, not a retrieval fix.
findings.detail: Quote: 'we conducted a critical ablation study comparing our hierarchical writer against a brute-force baseline that attempts to include the entire memory bank to generate the final report in multiple steps... The most striking improvements are in insight (42.72 → 50.02) and readability (43.82 → 49.79), which directly validates our hypothesis on attentional management; by focusing the model on a curated context for each section, it can perform deeper reasoning rather than shallow summarization.' The mechanism: for each outline section the writer (1) issues a targeted RETRIEVE of only that section's cited evidence from the memory bank, (2) runs an explicit THINK action ('analyzes the previous sections and retrieved content, synthesizes key insights, selects the most compelling pieces of evidence, and formulates a coherent narrative structure' — 'This internal monologue is critical for moving beyond simple summarization to genuine synthesis'), (3) emits prose in <write> tags, then (4) PRUNES that section's sources from context and replaces them with a placeholder before the next section. The prune step is what prevents 'contextual bleeding'. POLARIS writes 12 monolithic 633-word paragraphs — this is the brute-force baseline that scores 42.72 insight.

findings.claim: RACE's ArticleCleaner strips ONLY citations/references/footnotes and is explicitly instructed to preserve everything else — so markdown tables, H2/H3 headers and bullets DO survive to the judge. POLARIS's plan to add structure is safe.
findings.detail: The actual cleaning prompt (prompt/clean_prompt.py): 'Cleaning goal: remove all citation links, citation marks (such as [1], [2]...), reference lists, footnotes, and ensure the content reads smoothly. KEEP ALL OTHER ORIGINAL CONTENT; REMOVE ONLY CITATIONS.' Special case: a chunk that is entirely a bibliography returns empty string. Note pipeline v2 (May 2026) made cleaning CHUNK-BASED for long articles ('using a chunk-based strategy to better support very long articles'), operating per-fragment with no cross-chunk context — a minor risk that a table straddling a chunk boundary gets mangled, but headers/bullets/tables are not targets of removal. This confirms POLARIS's verified finding (citations invisible) AND extends it: structure is NOT invisible, it is fully visible.

findings.claim: RACE is a PAIRWISE side-by-side judgment in a single prompt, and the target is ALWAYS article_1 while the human reference is ALWAYS article_2 — there is no position randomization or order counterbalancing.
findings.detail: deepresearch_bench_race.py lines 101-102 pass `article_1=target_article, article_2=reference_article` with no shuffle/swap anywhere. The score prompt puts BOTH full articles in one context and instructs: 'Please strictly evaluate and compare <article_1> and <article_2> based on EACH criterion... 2. Comparative Evaluation: Analyze how the two articles perform on each criterion... 3. Score Separately: score each article on each criterion (0-10 continuous).' Anchors: 0-2 very poor, 2-4 poor, 4-6 average, 6-8 good, 8-10 excellent. Consequence: this is a CONTRAST judgment, not an absolute one. The judge writes a comparative 'analysis' field BEFORE emitting scores for each criterion, so a structurally impoverished article (0 subsections, 0 tables, 0 bullets next to a reference with 24 H3s, 10 tables, 91 bullets) gets that contrast narrated into the analysis text on EVERY criterion — including insight criteria — and drags scores that are nominally about analysis depth. Position is fixed, so it is a constant across all systems (not an exploitable lever, but it means measured deltas aren't position noise).

findings.claim: Confirmed aggregation: overall = target_total/(target_total+reference_total), and EACH dimension is independently normalized as target_dim/(target_dim+ref_dim). Both are weighted sums of per-criterion 0-10 scores using LLM-generated per-task criterion weights.
findings.detail: utils/score_calculator.py + deepresearch_bench_race.py lines 155-170: 'overall_score = target_total / (target_total + reference_total)' and 'normalized_dims[dim] = target_score / (target_score + reference_score)'. Weighted scores = sum over criteria of (criterion_weight * raw 0-10 score), then dimension_weight applied. Practical implication for POLARIS: insight 0.4238 means target_insight ≈ 0.735 × reference_insight. If the human reference scores ~8/10 on insight criteria, POLARIS is scoring ~5.9/10 — i.e. sitting in the '4-6 = average, basically meets the criterion, neither good nor bad' band. Getting to parity (0.50) requires moving insight criteria from the 'average' band to the 'good' (6-8) band, not a wholesale rewrite.

findings.claim: The judge's INSIGHT rubric explicitly rewards exactly the sentence types POLARIS's span-grounding forbids: causal mechanisms, second-order/non-obvious effects, cross-source synthesis, challenging conventional wisdom, and forward-looking scenarios. 'Information listing' is explicitly named as the anti-pattern.
findings.detail: criteria_prompt_en.py, generate_eval_criteria_prompt_insight: 'Beyond Surface-Level: Criteria should assess analytical depth, logical rigor, originality of insights, and value of conclusions, NOT JUST INFORMATION LISTING.' The canonical exemplar rubric it few-shots the judge with: (1) 'Depth of Analysis of Core Drivers and Mechanisms' 0.20 — 'deeply analyzes their INTERPLAY AND CAUSAL MECHANISMS, rather than a superficial listing'; (2) 'Sophistication in Uncovering Market Impacts' 0.20 — 'goes beyond obvious impacts to uncover SUBTLE OR SECOND-ORDER EFFECTS'; (3) 'Logical Coherence and Justification' 0.20 — recommendations 'logically DERIVED FROM the impact analysis'; (4) 'Nuance in Risk-Return Analysis' 0.15; (5) 'Originality and Strategic Value' 0.15 — 'unique perspectives, CHALLENGES CONVENTIONAL WISDOM... beyond common knowledge'; (6) 'Forward-Looking Perspective' 0.10. Every one of these six is a cross-source inference or a claim that no single span supports. A pipeline that span-grounds every sentence is structurally incapable of scoring above the 4-6 'average' band here. This is the mechanism behind insight being POLARIS's worst dimension at its heaviest weight (0.32).

findings.claim: The judge's READABILITY rubric explicitly scores heading levels and the presence of TABLES/CHARTS as a named criterion. POLARIS's readability 0.3774 is below EVERY system on the GPT-5.5 leaderboard (worst listed: grok at 45.72) — it is POLARIS's single most anomalous number and the cheapest to fix.
findings.detail: criteria_prompt_en.py readability exemplar criteria: 'Overall Article Structure and Logical Flow' (weight 0.25) — 'clear logical structure... with DISTINCT HEADING LEVELS, effectively guiding the reader'; and 'Information Presentation' — 'Is key information highlighted? Is data presented clearly, WITH APPROPRIATE USE OF CHARTS AND TABLES to illustrate trends and comparisons?'; plus 'Paragraph Cohesion' (0.15) — 'each paragraph focuses on a SINGLE IDEA'. POLARIS's 12 paragraphs × 633 words with 0 H3 / 0 tables / 0 bullets fails three named criteria simultaneously and mechanically. Even though readability carries the lowest task-72 weight (0.14), a 0.377→0.50 move is worth ~+0.017 overall on its own — and because the judge is pairwise/contrastive (see other finding), the structural deficit also bleeds into the insight and comprehensiveness analyses.

findings.claim: ADORE's actual method is a 5-agent hub-and-spoke pipeline whose central idea — 'memory-locked synthesis' — is a HARDER span-grounding constraint than POLARIS already has. ADORE is therefore the wrong system to copy for an insight problem; its own paper contains no ablations and does not name its LLM.
findings.detail: ADORE = Atlassian (You, Sun, Bora, Li, Goel, Li, Culatana; arXiv 2601.18267, 26 Jan 2026, SIGIR'26 submission). Agents: Orchestrator (routes fast-RAG vs deep-research by 'research complexity'); Grounding (clarification questions to fix underspecified prompts); Planning (sections/priorities/success criteria as a user-editable shared artifact); Execution (Self-Evolution Engine evolves queries from detected evidence gaps); Report Generation (initial outline → outline revision on retrieved evidence → final report); WebSearch. Three contributions: (1) Memory-locked synthesis — 'each section is written using ONLY the section-scoped evidence stored in the claim–evidence graph... traceability by construction'; (2) Evidence-coverage-guided execution — section-level coverage audits drive targeted re-retrieval, stopping on coverage+citation-stability rather than a fixed iteration budget; (3) Section-packed long-context grounding — pack only section-relevant evidence per generation call, prune low-salience text, compress sources into citation-preserving summaries. CRITICAL READ: (1) is a STRICTER admissible-evidence constraint than POLARIS's span-grounding, and RACE strips citations anyway, so ADORE's headline contribution buys POLARIS nothing on RACE. Its real, transferable content is (2)+(3), which are re-derivations of WebWeaver. The paper has NO ablation table, names no backbone model, and its RACE numbers (52.65 / comp 52.22 / insight 54.37 / IF 51.11 / read 52.18) are self-reported under the legacy judge.

findings.claim: WebWeaver — not ADORE — is the real, reproducible ancestor: it has a paper, full ablations, AND open-source code, and ADORE explicitly cites it as its inspiration. WebWeaver (Claude-Sonnet-4) = 50.58 overall / insight 50.02, i.e. it BEATS the human reference on insight from an open pipeline.
findings.detail: WebWeaver (Li et al., Tongyi Lab / Alibaba, arXiv 2509.13312). Code: https://github.com/Alibaba-NLP/DeepResearch/tree/main/WebAgent/WebWeaver (ReAct implemented via Qwen-Agent). Dual-agent: PLANNER with action space {search, write_outline, terminate} runs a dynamic cycle interleaving evidence acquisition and outline optimization — search results are (a) distilled into a query-relevant SUMMARY fed back into planner context and (b) mined for verifiable EVIDENCE (quotes, data points) stored in a memory bank; the outline is repeatedly restructured and populated with citation IDs pointing INTO the memory bank. WRITER then does hierarchical retrieve→think→write→prune per section. RACE (legacy judge): WebWeaver(Claude-Sonnet-4) 50.58 / comp 51.45 / insight 50.02 / IF 50.81 / read 49.79, citation accuracy 93.37%; WebWeaver(Qwen3-235B) 50.62 / insight 51.00. Both exceed 50 = the human reference, which is the exact bar POLARIS is trying to clear. ADORE's own related-work says: 'WebWeaver emphasizes outline-centric evidence structuring and section-level writing with a memory bank.'

findings.claim: WebWeaver's second ablation: iterative OUTLINE OPTIMIZATION (vs a one-shot outline) is worth comp 48.85→50.82 and insight 46.33→48.35. The outline is not scaffolding — it is where the insight is actually manufactured.
findings.detail: Quote: 'On DeepResearch Bench, the overall score steadily climbs, driven primarily by significant gains in comprehensiveness (48.85 → 50.82) and insight (46.33 → 48.35).' They also judge the OUTLINE ITSELF with a separate rubric and show its quality rises monotonically 81.9 → 92.3 over optimization rounds, with the 'Support' sub-score (mapping of planned sections to available evidence) rising 51.2 → 73.6. Their outline-judgement rubric (Appendix A) scores Depth ('mechanisms/causal drivers, assumptions and uncertainties... where evidence, examples, and VISUALS will be integrated'), Balance ('counterarguments... trade-offs, limitations, counter-evidence'), Breadth ('historical, legal/regulatory, economic, technical, ethical, geographic/comparative, stakeholder, risks, implementation pathways'), and Insightfulness ('non-obvious connections... propose suitable EXHIBITS (tables, charts, frameworks) with a clear purpose'). Note how closely that outline rubric mirrors the RACE insight+readability rubrics — optimizing the outline against these dimensions front-loads the score.

findings.claim: Concrete shape of a winning report, measured: ~26,000 output tokens written in ~25 DISCRETE writing steps (i.e. ~25 sections, ~1k tokens each), from a ~4,900-token outline refined over ~2.2 optimization rounds, backed by ~112 saved pages / ~67k evidence tokens gathered over ~15.7 search steps and ~20 unique queries.
findings.detail: WebWeaver Table 3 (Claude-Sonnet-4 on DeepResearch Bench): #search steps 15.71, #outline tokens 4876.21, #outline optimizations 2.16, #saved pages 112.25, #search queries 20.24, #evidence tokens 67,237, #summary tokens 14,980, #output tokens 26,127, #writing steps 24.78. Paper's gloss: 'the writer's process of composing a 26k-token report in 25 discrete writing steps validates that our hierarchical synthesis is a practical way to maintain coherence over long outputs.' Compare POLARIS: 12 paragraphs, one monolithic composition pass. The human reference (59 paragraphs, 24 H3, 10 tables, 91 bullets) is structurally much closer to the 25-section WebWeaver shape than to POLARIS's. The '~25 writing steps' is the number to engineer toward — one LLM call per H3.

findings.claim: Insight is the dimension that separates winners from losers on BOTH leaderboards — it has the widest spread and top systems' insight EXCEEDS their other dimensions, while losing systems' insight is their WORST dimension.
findings.detail: GPT-5.5 board, insight minus readability: cellcog-max +5.14, lunon +4.35, WhaleCloud +2.85, bodhi +2.73, sourcery +2.54 (all winners, positive) vs openai-DR -0.93, perplexity -4.76, grok -7.60 (all losers, negative). Insight range across the board is 38.12→57.08 (19.0 pts) vs readability 45.72→53.20 (only 7.5 pts). Legacy board is even starker: insight range 20.44→61.48 (41 pts). So insight is where the variance lives AND it carries the heaviest task-72 weight (0.32). POLARIS's insight-minus-readability is +4.64, which superficially looks like a 'winner' profile — but that is an artifact of an abnormally LOW readability (37.74, below every listed system), not a high insight. Both dimensions are depressed; insight is depressed at 3x the weight.

findings.claim: A third, distinct composition paradigm worth knowing: TTD-DR (Google) writes a deliberately BAD full draft first from parametric knowledge, then 'denoises' it with retrieval — the evolving draft steers the next search query. Plus component-wise self-evolution with an LLM-judge fitness score on every stage.
findings.detail: TTD-DR (arXiv 2507.16075, Google). Report generation as a diffusion process: 'we prompt an LLM to generate an initial draft report based on the user's query. This draft serves as a NOISY starting point.' Algorithm 1: feed current draft into the search-question generator → retrieve → synthesize answer → 'the new information is used to revise the report draft, either by adding new details or by verifying existing information' → loop. Explicitly contrasted with per-section search: 'TTD-DR AVOIDS SEPARATED SEARCHES FOR EACH SECTION TO MAINTAIN GLOBAL CONTEXT.' Second mechanism, Self-Evolution: each stage agent (plan, question, answer, report) spawns multiple variants, each scored by an LLM-as-a-judge auto-rater on Helpfulness/Comprehensiveness which returns BOTH a fitness score and a textual critique, variants are revised on the critique, then merged. Reported: 74.5% win rate vs OpenAI DR on long-form. NOTE: TTD-DR reports DeepConsult/HLE/GAIA — I did NOT find a RACE score for it, so it is not directly rankable here. Its self-evolution loop (generate variants → LLM-judge critique → revise → merge) is the most direct known way to optimize prose AGAINST a rubric, which is what POLARIS needs for the insight criteria.

findings.claim: Tavily's SOTA claim is real but its public write-up contains no usable composition mechanism — it is an engineering/token-efficiency story, not a report-writing story. Do not spend more time on it.
findings.detail: tavily-research = 52.44 on the legacy board (comp 52.84 / insight 53.59 / IF 51.92 / read 49.21 — note readability is its weakest dim and it is absent from the GPT-5.5 board). The blog's substantive claims are all about retrieval and context economy: Advanced Search returns 'only the most relevant content chunks from each source'; 'global state persistence and source deduplication' to keep the agent on fresh info and detect when 'the information scope is narrowing'; 66% token reduction vs Open Deep Research; 'guard-railed freedom' for the LLM to iterate. On composition it says only that raw information should be provided as context 'once your agent begins to prepare the final deliverable... to ensure there is no information loss' — which is the monolithic brute-force pattern WebWeaver ablates AGAINST, and is consistent with Tavily's low readability score. No outline, no section-wise writing, no critique loop disclosed.

findings.claim: Cross-judge scores are not comparable, and the benchmark now also has a successor (DRB II). Any number quoted for a system without naming its evaluator is untrustworthy — this is the trap that produced both the 48.88 and the 52.65 errors.
findings.detail: Repo news, 11 May 2026: official evaluator switched Gemini-2.5-Pro → GPT-5.5 (FACT pipeline → GPT-5.4-mini), chosen by alignment with human judgments on a 50-task × 4-DRA annotated subset against a human inter-annotator agreement baseline of 68.78% (GPT-5.5 71.82 overall / PAR 73.00 / OPC 89.70 / FAP 65.35 / FAS 59.23, beating Gemini-3.1-Pro 70.58 and Claude-Opus-4-7 70.11). Two leaderboards were maintained in parallel during a dual-acceptance window to 31 May 2026; after 1 June only the GPT-5.5 board is maintained. The 48.88 figure POLARIS was previously burned by is the STATIC table still sitting on deepresearch-bench.github.io (gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch, old judge) — that page is stale and should be treated as poisoned. ADORE's 52.65 is the same class of error one generation later. Also note DeepResearch Bench II shipped 6 Feb 2026 (arXiv 2601.08536) with a different evaluation focus, though DRB continues to be maintained.

findings.claim: Section-by-section writing (outline first, then compose exactly one section per session with only that section's evidence in context) is the single most-validated lever for INSIGHT on RACE. FS-Researcher's ablation shows removing it costs -8.80 Insight and -5.13 overall RACE.
findings.detail:
FS-Researcher (ACL 2026, arXiv 2602.01566) Table 5, GPT-5 backbone, Gemini-2.5-Pro RACE judge:
  FS-Researcher (GPT-5): Comp 51.96 | Insight 54.44 | Instr 52.14 | Read 51.26 | RACE 52.76
  - Section-wise Writing (i.e. one-shot report generation instead): Comp 47.06 | Insight 45.64 | Instr 50.50 | Read 46.46 | RACE 47.63
Deltas: Insight -8.80, Comp -4.90, Read -4.80, Instr -1.64, RACE -5.13. Insight is BY FAR the most sensitive dimension to this one change — it moves ~1.7x more than comprehensiveness and ~5x more than instruction-following.
Mechanism as implemented: 'Report Writer creates an outline file in the first writing session, and chooses exactly one section to compose in subsequent sessions.' The writer is a separate agent from the searcher and reads a persistent file-system knowledge base as its source of facts. This is directly actionable for POLARIS: you currently emit 12 undifferentiated 633-word paragraphs from (presumably) one pass; the ablation says one-shot generation is exactly the configuration that destroys Insight.
CAVEAT: judge is Gemini-2.5-Pro, not gpt-5.5. Absolute values are not comparable to your 0.4382; the DELTA is the transferable claim.

findings.claim: The mechanism behind the insight gain is CONTEXT CURATION PER SECTION, not the outline itself. WebWeaver's hierarchical (per-section retrieval) writing beats brute-force (all evidence in one context) by +7.30 Insight and +5.97 Readability.
findings.detail:
WebWeaver (Alibaba Tongyi, arXiv 2509.13312v3), DeepResearch Bench, Gemini-2.5-pro judge, Claude-sonnet-4 backbone:
  Hierarchical retrieval+writing: Overall 50.58 | Comp 51.45 | Insight 50.02 | Instr 50.81 | Read 49.79 | citation acc 93.37
  Brute-force ('LongWriter'-style, dump everything, write in one go): Insight 42.72 | Read 43.82 | citation acc 86.73
Deltas: Insight +7.30, Readability +5.97, CitAcc +6.64.
The paper's stated explanation is the key sentence for POLARIS: 'By focusing the model on a curated context for each section, it can perform deeper reasoning rather than shallow summarization.' This is precisely your diagnosed failure ('we LIST findings; the reference EXPLAINS them'). The claim is that shallow listing is a CONTEXT-DILUTION artifact: when a writer holds N sources for the whole report it enumerates; when it holds the 5-10 sources relevant to one subsection it compares and reasons across them. Note this reproduces the FS-Researcher result independently, with a different backbone and a different codebase.
Secondary ablation: outline optimization rounds raise Insight 46.33 -> 48.35 (iteratively interleaving evidence acquisition with outline revision, i.e. the outline is NOT frozen up front). Output ~26k tokens over ~25 writing steps.
Repo: https://github.com/Alibaba-NLP/DeepResearch/tree/main/WebAgent/WebWeaver

findings.claim: A persistent external knowledge base (file system) that the WRITER reads — separate from the searcher's trajectory — is worth another -7.95 Insight when removed. Merging searcher and writer into one agent is catastrophic: -16.89 Insight.
findings.detail:
Same FS-Researcher Table 5 (GPT-5, Gemini judge):
  - Persistent Workspace removed: Comp 48.38 | Insight 46.49 | Instr 50.78 | Read 49.92 | RACE 48.69  (Insight -7.95, RACE -4.07)
  - Dual-Agent merged into a single agent that browses and writes: Comp 40.90 | Insight 37.55 | Instr 46.30 | Read 44.78 | RACE 42.41  (Insight -16.89, RACE -10.35)
The dual-agent ablation is the biggest single effect in any RACE ablation I found, and again Insight absorbs the most damage. Architecture: a 'Context Builder' agent browses, writes structured notes, and archives raw sources into a hierarchical KB that can exceed the context window; a separate 'Report Writer' agent treats the KB as ground truth and composes section by section. Rationale is context-budget: in a single agent, the search trajectory eats the token budget that the writing needs, so the writer never has room to reason.
Relevance to POLARIS: your span-grounding pipeline is effectively a merged searcher/writer that emits sentences bound to spans. The ablation predicts that decoupling — build a notes KB, then have a fresh writer agent synthesize FROM the notes with no obligation to span-ground each sentence — is where the insight sentences become possible at all.

findings.claim: Test-time scaling on the RESEARCH side (more context-builder rounds) raises Insight monotonically but readability peaks early — more search rounds is an insight lever, not a readability lever.
findings.detail:
FS-Researcher Figure 4 (10 sampled queries, varying Context Builder round budget):
  3 rounds: 21.6 sources, 17.1 notes -> RACE 51.18 | Comp 49.72 | Insight 52.27 | Read 51.43
  5 rounds: 33.3 sources, 18.0 notes -> RACE 52.37 | Comp 51.96 | Insight 53.43 | Read 51.93
 10 rounds: 38.8 sources, 20.5 notes -> RACE 53.05 | Comp 52.31 | Insight 54.70 | Read 51.66
Insight climbs +2.43 from 3->10 rounds; readability peaks at 5 and then declines. Report length grows 21,306 -> 29,244 characters (~3.5k-5k words) across the budget sweep. Note the absolute report length here is SHORTER than your 7,692 cleaned words — so the SOTA systems are not winning on raw length. This is a caution against 'write more words' as the fix.

findings.claim: STORM is a NEGATIVE result on DeepResearch Bench — it is the worst multi-agent baseline measured, scoring BELOW a plain ReAct single agent and below a naive prompt. Do not port STORM.
findings.detail:
ScaffoldAgent (arXiv 2606.20122) Table 1, DeepResearch Bench RACE (Over./Comp/Ins./Inst/Read):
  DeepSeek-V3.2 backbone: naive Prompt 43.68/41.77/41.67/47.21/46.35; ReAct 43.26/42.70/40.53/46.10/46.43; STORM 35.26/33.69/31.52/39.02/39.07; WebWeaver 43.52/41.27/42.37/47.18/44.91; ScaffoldAgent 48.27/46.40/49.67/48.98/47.31
  Qwen3-32B backbone: STORM 32.81/30.63/31.66/34.88/36.90 (also last).
STORM's Insight is 31.5-31.7, roughly 10 points below the naive prompt baseline. STORM was designed for Wikipedia-style articles, and its perspective-guided QA + rigid outline appears to actively hurt on RACE's open-ended research tasks. Given how frequently STORM is recommended as 'the' outline-then-expand method, this is worth knowing before you spend a night on it.

findings.claim: Outline CONTRACTION and REVISION (deleting/merging sections, not just adding them) are what make outline optimization work. 'Expansion-only' outlining costs -6.54 RACE. This directly contradicts your Rank10 finding that 'more sections' is exhausted — the lever is pruning, not adding.
findings.detail:
ScaffoldAgent (arXiv 2606.20122), DeepSeek-V3.2, Table 3 (Action ablation):
  Full: RACE 48.27 | Eff.cit 51.18 | CitAcc 62.20
  w/o Contraction: RACE 44.72 (-3.55)
  w/o Revision:    RACE 43.09 (-5.18)
  Expansion-only:  RACE 41.73 (-6.54)
Table 4 (Utility ablation — the outline is scored by a utility function with three terms):
  w/o U_ret (retrieval utility):   45.42 (-2.85)
  w/o U_str (structural utility):  44.17 (-4.10)
  w/o U_gen (generation utility):  43.58 (-4.69)
ScaffoldAgent's Insight (49.67 on DeepSeek-V3.2) is its single best dimension and beats the next-best system (StackPlanner 46.29) by +3.38, while its readability advantage is small — i.e. dynamic utility-guided outlining is specifically an INSIGHT lever.
Median report length ~22K characters (~3.3k words) — again, notably SHORTER than your reports.
CAVEAT: judge model is not named in the paper (I could not verify it), and backbones are weak (Qwen3-32B / DeepSeek-V3.2), so absolute numbers run ~5 points low. Confidence medium on the exact figures, high on the sign/ordering.

findings.claim: The 'draft first, then deepen' interleaving (WARP) is the only published method whose Insight (52.64) exceeds its own comprehensiveness (50.54) and readability (44.17) by a wide margin — it is an explicit depth-over-coverage trade, and it is trained, not prompted.
findings.detail:
AgentCPM-Report (arXiv 2602.06540), 'WARP: Writing As Reasoning Policy', DeepResearch Bench, Gemini-2.5-Pro judge:
  Final (Pipeline RL): Overall 50.11 | Comp 50.54 | Insight 52.64 | Instr 48.87 | Read 44.17
Training-stage ablation (Comp / Insight / Instr / Read):
  SFT:       46.24 / 48.10 / 48.10 / 41.79
  Atomic RL: 48.81 / 48.70 / 51.36 / 48.64
  Pipeline RL: 50.54 / 52.64 / 52.64 / 48.87
Insight moves +4.54 from SFT to Pipeline RL — the largest dimension gain from training.
Mechanism (prompt-portable even if you can't train): the outline is NOT frozen up front. The agent alternates (a) evidence-based drafting of a section, then (b) 'reasoning-driven deepening' — re-reading the condensed intermediate draft to extract insights, identify missing topics, and decide which sections need EXPANSION. Structure statistics of its reports: ~6.5 H1, ~15.75 H2, ~17.32 H3 (compare: your 0 subsections; the human reference's 24 H3). The trained model issues ~8.6 'expand' actions vs 4.44 for SFT — i.e. the learned behavior is literally 'go back and deepen this section'.
Note its readability (44.17) is its weak spot, mirroring yours (0.3774) — depth and readability appear to trade off unless structure is explicitly enforced.

findings.claim: The RACE judge's Insight criterion explicitly rewards NON-OBVIOUS IMPACTS, causal/trend analysis, originality and forward-looking strategic value — not evidence coverage. Your span-grounded, one-sentence-per-span construction is structurally incapable of emitting the sentence type being scored.
findings.detail:
From the benchmark's own judge prompts (github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench, prompt/criteria_prompt_en.py and prompt/score_prompt_en.py):
  - Insight/Depth is defined as 'the depth, originality, logic, and value of the analysis and conclusions' — moving beyond surface-level data.
  - Criteria generation is instructed to assess whether the article 'uncovers non-obvious impacts' and demonstrates 'logical rigor', valuing 'originality and strategic value', forward-looking perspectives, and risk-return sophistication 'rather than descriptive content alone'.
  - Scoring: BOTH articles get an independent continuous 0-10 per criterion. 8-10 = 'fully meets or exceeds'; 4-6 = 'basically meets, neither good nor bad'. Overall = weighted sum of dimension scores, weights sum to 1.0 and are generated per task.
  - Confirmed: the scoring prompt places NO constraint or reward on length, citations, or structural elements. Consistent with your ArticleCleaner finding — citations are invisible.
Actionable read: to move Insight you must add sentences that state a CAUSE, a CONSEQUENCE, a TREND, a TRADE-OFF, or a NON-OBVIOUS implication, and these by definition cannot be grounded in a single span. The published fix in every system above is the same: separate the writer from the retriever so the writer is allowed to synthesize across the notes.

findings.claim: WARNING — JUDGE MISMATCH. Nearly every published RACE number (FS-Researcher 53.94, WebWeaver 50.58, RhinoInsight 50.92, AgentCPM 50.11) was scored by Gemini-2.5-Pro, NOT the gpt-5.5 evaluator you are pinned to. Treat published absolute scores as non-comparable to your 0.4382; only the ablation deltas transfer.
findings.detail:
The benchmark README's News section: following Google's announced June 17, 2026 deprecation of Gemini-2.5-Pro, the maintainers benchmarked three frontier reasoning models and adopted GPT-5.5 as the official RACE evaluator (71.82% alignment with 200 human-annotated articles, vs a 68.78% human inter-annotator baseline). Migration: dual-acceptance window through May 31 2026 with two separate leaderboards ('rankings remain directly comparable within each evaluator'); by June 1 2026 the original-paper results were re-evaluated under GPT-5.5 and migrated; after that, GPT-5.5 submissions only.
Consequences: (a) the project website deepresearch-bench.github.io STILL shows the legacy Gemini-judge table (Gemini-2.5-Pro DR = 48.88, OpenAI DR = 46.98, Perplexity 42.25, Grok 40.24) — this is the exact stale 48.88 you were burned by; do not use that page. (b) ADORE's 52.65 (arXiv 2601.18267, Jan 2026) is a leaderboard rank-1 claim, and since the maintainers re-scored migrated entries under GPT-5.5, the 52.65 you are targeting is plausibly GPT-5.5 — but the ADORE PAPER itself never names its judge model, so I cannot verify this. Flagging explicitly rather than guessing.

findings.claim: ADORE (current SOTA, 52.65) has NO published ablation table. Its highest dimension is Insight (54.37) and its mechanism is a Claim-Evidence Graph memory bank + section-scoped admissible evidence — architecturally the same family as FS-Researcher/WebWeaver, so its gain is likely the same lever.
findings.detail:
ADORE = 'Orchestrating Specialized Agents for Trustworthy Enterprise RAG' (arXiv 2601.18267). Leaderboard rank #1: Overall 52.65 | Comp 52.22 | Insight 54.37 | Instr 51.11 | Read 52.18. Also 77.2% head-to-head win rate on DeepConsult.
What the paper actually says: a central orchestrator + specialized agents; generation is constrained to a structured Memory Bank holding explicit claim-evidence linkage PER SECTION; each planned section tracks 'the admissible evidence set (sources and excerpts) and coverage requirements'; the Report Generation Agent drafts an outline, a Self-Evolution Engine does iterative retrieval, the outline is UPDATED with new evidence, and final synthesis writes each section 'using only the section-scoped evidence'.
What the paper does NOT contain (I read it): no ablation tables, no component-removal analysis, no judge model disclosed, no report-length/structure/table statistics, and no explanation of HOW cross-source insight is produced. So the 54.37 Insight is unexplained by the authors. Its architecture is section-scoped-evidence + dynamic outline + separate writer — i.e. the exact configuration the FS-Researcher and WebWeaver ablations isolate as the insight lever. Note the tension for POLARIS: ADORE also enforces strict evidence-grounding per section, so grounding per se is not the problem — grounding per SECTION (a bundle of sources the writer may reason across) rather than per SENTENCE/SPAN is the distinction.

findings.claim: Checklist-then-critic control (RhinoInsight) reaches 50.92 RACE with Insight 51.45, but its published ablation is on DeepConsult (1-10 scale), NOT on RACE — do not plan against a RACE delta for it.
findings.detail:
RhinoInsight (arXiv 2511.18743), DeepResearch Bench, Gemini-judge era: Overall 50.92 | Comp 50.51 | Insight 51.45 | Instr 51.72 | Read 50.00.
Two modules: (1) Verification Checklist Module — turns the query into traceable verifiable sub-goals, refined by an LLM critic, compiled into a hierarchical outline that anchors actions; (2) Evidence Audit Module — structures search content, iteratively updates the outline, PRUNES noisy context, and binds evidence to claims via critic ranking.
Ablation (DeepConsult, avg score /10, NOT RACE): no modules 3.65 -> VCM only 5.31 -> EAM only 5.45 -> both 6.82. So the critic/checklist story is real but its RACE-dimension attribution is unpublished. FS-Researcher reports beating RhinoInsight as the strongest baseline by +3.02 RACE (53.94 vs 50.92), which is consistent and cross-validates both numbers.
The transferable bit is the CONTEXT PRUNING claim — noisy context is treated as the enemy of depth, same thesis as WebWeaver's curated per-section context.

findings.claim: TTD-DR (Google 'test-time diffusion', arXiv 2507.16075) is frequently cited as a deep-research SOTA but it does NOT report DeepResearch Bench / RACE at all. Its ablation is on win-rate and correctness. Any RACE number attributed to it is fabricated.
findings.detail:
I read the paper HTML. It contains Table 1 (LongForm Research, DeepConsult, HLE-Search, HLE-Full, GAIA), Table 2 (ablation: Backbone DR Agent -> +Self-evolution -> +Diffusion with Retrieval), Table 4 (hyperparameters). Metrics are Win Rate and Correctness. There is no DeepResearch Bench table and no comprehensiveness/insight/instruction/readability breakdown. Headline numbers are 69.1% win rate on LongForm Research and 74.5% on DeepConsult.
The method itself (start from a noisy preliminary draft skeleton, iteratively 'denoise' it with retrieval at each step; component-wise self-evolution on planning/query-gen/synthesis) is conceptually the same 'draft-then-deepen' family as AgentCPM's WARP, which DOES have RACE numbers. If you want the draft-then-deepen lever with a RACE number attached, cite AgentCPM (Insight 48.10 -> 52.64), not TTD-DR.

findings.claim: Report LENGTH is a weak, confounded lever and the SOTA systems are SHORTER than POLARIS already. There is no published length-vs-RACE correlation; the r=0.20-0.28 length correlation floating around is from ResearchRubrics, a different benchmark with a different (atomic-rubric) protocol.
findings.detail:
Length data I could verify: FS-Researcher reports 21,306-29,244 CHARACTERS (~3.5k-5k words) across its round budgets. ScaffoldAgent's median is ~22K characters (~3.3k words). WebWeaver produces ~26k tokens over ~25 writing steps. POLARIS's cleaned report is 7,692 words — i.e. you are already LONGER than every system that beats you. The human reference is 59 paragraphs x 142 words ~= 8.4k words, so length parity is already achieved.
The 'longer = higher score' correlation (r ~= 0.20-0.28 for Gemini/ChatGPT DR agents) comes from ResearchRubrics (arXiv 2511.07685), whose own authors attribute it to length-quality CONFLATION (longer reports mechanically satisfy more atomic rubric items), not to a verbosity preference — and ResearchRubrics is not RACE. I could not find any RACE-specific length-bias study; I checked arXiv 2512.01948 ('How Far Are We from Genuinely Useful Deep Research Agents?') and arXiv 2606.01629 and neither contains the analysis. Marking this LOW confidence deliberately: your Rank9 finding (8055w, density up) and this literature both point the same way — length is not the remaining 0.088.

findings.claim: Convergent recommendation across all four systems with real ablations: the highest-expected-value change for POLARIS is to split the pipeline into (1) a notes/KB builder and (2) a fresh writer agent that composes ONE H2/H3 subsection at a time from a section-scoped evidence bundle, with a dynamic outline that can contract and revise.
findings.detail:
Four independent ablations, four different codebases, four different backbones, all isolate the same lever and all show Insight as the most sensitive dimension:
  - FS-Researcher: remove section-wise writing -> Insight -8.80; merge searcher+writer -> Insight -16.89; remove persistent KB -> Insight -7.95.
  - WebWeaver: per-section curated context vs one-shot -> Insight +7.30, Readability +5.97.
  - ScaffoldAgent: outline must contract/revise, not just expand -> expansion-only costs -6.54 RACE; Insight is its standout dimension (+3.38 over next best).
  - AgentCPM/WARP: draft-then-deepen with expand actions -> Insight +4.54 over SFT; produces ~6.5 H1 / ~15.75 H2 / ~17.32 H3.
The structural signature of every winner (many H2/H3, per-section evidence bundles, ~3-5k words) is close to your human reference's profile (24 H3, 142-word paragraphs) and far from yours (0 subsections, 633-word paragraphs). Note this ALSO explains your readability floor (0.3774): 12 monolithic paragraphs with no subsections is a direct hit to 'clarity of structure' and 'effectiveness of data presentation', which the RACE readability criterion names explicitly — so subsectioning + tables should move readability (0.14 weight) and insight (0.32 weight) simultaneously. Expected combined ceiling from the literature's deltas is well above your 0.088 gap, but I have no RACE evidence for tables/bullets specifically — no paper ablates formatting elements, so treat the readability half as untested.
findings.system: synthesis across FS-Researcher / WebWeaver / ScaffoldAgent / AgentCPM

findings.claim: RACE's readability criteria for task 72 EXPLICITLY name the artifacts POLARIS is missing — including, verbatim, 'a summary table of key studies and their findings' — so tables/headings are directly scored, not merely implied.
findings.detail: Pulled the pinned criteria for id=72 straight from data/criteria_data/criteria.jsonl. Readability (dim weight 0.14) decomposes into 7 criteria: L1 Language Clarity (0.20), S1 Overall Structure & Logical Organization (0.20) — 'logically sequenced thematic sections with informative headings that reflect the facets of AI's impact on labor'; S2 Paragraph Cohesion & Transitions (0.15) — 'Evaluates if each paragraph focuses on a single, clear idea'; P1 Clarity and Synthesis in Presenting Sourced Information (0.15); D1 Clarity of Data/Evidence (0.10) — 'If the review itself uses tables or figures to compile or synthesize information from sources (e.g., a summary table of key studies and their findings), assesses their clarity, labeling, and effectiveness'; F1 Formatting/Layout/Visual Consistency (0.10) — 'heading styles', 'clear paragraph breaks'; A1 Audience Adaptation (0.10). Structure-bearing criteria (S1+S2+D1+F1) = 0.55 of the readability dimension. Our 0 tables / 0 subsections / 633-word paragraphs fail S1, S2, D1 and F1 simultaneously. Note D1 is conditional ('if the review itself uses tables') — a judge cannot award the table sub-score for a document with no tables, but it can and does award it to the human reference, which has 10.

findings.claim: The RACE ArticleCleaner strips ONLY citations/references/footnotes and is explicitly instructed to preserve everything else — so markdown headings, tables and bullets DO survive to the judge. Structure is visible; citations are not.
findings.detail: clean_prompt.py, clean_article_prompt_en, verbatim: 'Cleaning goal: remove all citation links, citation marks (such as [1], [2], ... ), reference lists, footnotes, and ensure the content reads smoothly. Keep all other original content; remove only citations.' This confirms your team's finding about citations AND establishes the complement: every structural token (##, |---|, -, **) reaches the judge intact. It also means task-72 readability criterion F1, which asks the judge to assess 'the visual consistency and unobtrusive integration of in-text citations and the clarity of the bibliography's formatting', is scoring something that has been deleted from BOTH articles — symmetric, so it neither helps nor hurts, but ~0.10 of readability is effectively noise.

findings.claim: Pure presentation CANNOT close your 0.088 gap. Because RACE overall = weighted_sum(target)/(weighted_sum(target)+weighted_sum(reference)), +1 raw judge point across ALL criteria is worth only ~+0.040-0.046 overall; and readability (w=0.14) taken all the way to human parity buys at most +0.018 to +0.024.
findings.detail: Read deepresearch_bench_race.py (lines 151-171) and utils/score_calculator.py. Scores are 0-10 per criterion (score_prompt_en.py: 'scoring each on a scale of 0-10'). overall = T/(T+R) where T=sum_d w_d * (criterion-weighted mean of target scores in dim d). Therefore d(overall)/d(T_d) = w_d * (1-p)/S, with p=0.4382 and S=T+R. Inverting your published numbers: if the human reference scores ~8/10 per dim, your implied raw scores are insight 5.88, comprehensiveness 6.68, instruction-following 6.31, readability 4.85; if reference scores ~7, yours are 5.15/5.84/5.52/4.24. Robust across that range: +1 point on EVERY criterion = +0.040 to +0.046 overall. Your 0.088 gap to ADORE therefore requires roughly +2.0 raw judge points, and your 0.062 gap to human parity requires ~+1.4. Per-dimension value of +1 raw point: insight +0.013-0.015, comprehensiveness +0.012-0.013, instruction-following +0.010-0.011, readability +0.006. Taking readability from its current implied 4.24-4.85 all the way to 8.0 (human parity) yields only +0.018 to +0.024 overall — i.e. maxing out presentation, scored where presentation is scored, recovers 20-27% of the gap. The other 73-80% must come from insight/comprehensiveness.

findings.claim: But formatting is NOT confined to readability — a readability criterion (P1, weight 0.15) explicitly penalizes the exact failure mode your span-grounding causes, so your 'we list, they explain' defect is double-counted across insight (0.32) AND readability (0.14).
findings.detail: Task-72 readability criterion P1 verbatim: 'Evaluates the effectiveness of synthesizing information from multiple sources to build a coherent understanding of topics ... rather than just serially summarizing individual papers.' That is the same construct as task-72's insight criteria: 'Critical Synthesis and Nuanced Evaluation ... rather than merely cataloging effects' (0.25) and 'Identification and Articulation of Emergent Themes, Theoretical Linkages, or Novel Perspectives' (0.20) and 'Analytical Depth in Characterizing ... the underlying *mechanisms*' (0.25). Combined exposure to cross-source inference: 0.32*(0.25+0.25+0.20) = 0.224 of the report score via insight, plus 0.14*0.15 = 0.021 via readability. This is the single largest addressable block in the rubric, and it is substance, not formatting.

findings.claim: The only RACE-native structure ablation in the literature — WebWeaver — shows that outline-driven, section-by-section writing with per-section evidence retrieval, vs. dumping all evidence in context and writing in one pass, moves INSIGHT by +7.30 RACE points and READABILITY by +5.97. That is nearly your entire 0.088 gap, and it comes from a generation-time structure change, not cosmetic markdown.
findings.detail: Verified verbatim in the raw HTML (not a snippet): 'The most striking improvements are in insight (42.72 -> 50.02) and readability (43.82 -> 49.79)' for hierarchical memory-grounded writing vs the brute-force all-context baseline; citation accuracy 86.73 -> 93.37. Table 1 verified: WebWeaver (Claude-sonnet-4) overall 50.58 / comp 51.45 / insight 50.02 / IF 50.81 / read 49.79. Separately, iterating the outline monotonically improves scores: 'On DeepResearch Bench, the overall score steadily climbs, driven primarily by significant gains in comprehensiveness (48.85 -> 50.82) and insight (46.33 -> 48.35)' across ~3 optimization rounds. Mechanism, from the paper: the Planner interleaves search with outline optimization; the Writer then, 'For each section of the outline, performs targeted retrieval of only the pertinent evidence from the memory bank using citations and composes the content', pruning materials from context before the next section. The causal story that fits your situation: writing one section at a time against a scoped evidence set is what PRODUCES cross-source comparison sentences, because the model is forced to reconcile several sources on one narrow claim instead of streaming spans. The +7.3 insight gain is an artifact of the writing procedure, not of adding headers.

findings.claim: Deflationary counter-evidence: on a rubric-based benchmark that is NOT style-susceptible, presentation is the ONE thing deep-research agents already do well — o3 Deep Research scores 89.16% on Presentation but 39.98% on Information Recall and 49.85% on Analysis. Formatting is not where the field loses to humans.
findings.detail: DeepResearch Bench II (verified verbatim in raw HTML, Table 'Main Results'): OpenAI-GPT-o3 Deep Research 39.98 / 49.85 / 89.16 / TotalScore 45.40; Gemini-3-Pro Deep Research 39.09 / 48.94 / 91.85 / 44.60; Gemini-2.5-Pro 34.91 / 51.91 / 90.x. Paper: 'even the strongest agents fail to pass more than 50% of the rubrics, with especially large deficits in Information Recall and Analysis.' ~71 binary rubrics per task extracted from expert articles, ~400 human-hours of review. IMPORTANT QUALIFIER FOR POLARIS: this says presentation is saturated for TYPICAL agents (which already emit headings/tables/bullets). POLARIS is an outlier — 0 tables, 0 subsections, 0 bullets, 633-word paragraphs — so you are almost certainly BELOW the 89-92% presentation norm, and part of your gap is a deficit other agents simply do not have. The benchmark also self-limits: 'the current presentation dimension primarily assesses the formatting and layout of reports'.

findings.claim: Calibration check against a shipped baseline: vanilla claude-3-7-sonnet scores 0.4316 overall on task 72 with readability 0.4245. POLARIS's readability of 0.3774 is BELOW an off-the-shelf single-model baseline — evidence that your formatting deficit is real and not just 'presentation doesn't matter'.
findings.detail: Extracted id=72 from the repo's shipped raw_results.jsonl for claude-3-7-sonnet-latest: comprehensiveness 0.4415, insight 0.4046, instruction_following 0.4564, readability 0.4245, overall 0.4316. POLARIS (0.4382 overall) beats it on insight (0.4238 vs 0.4046) and comprehensiveness (0.4549 vs 0.4415) but LOSES on readability (0.3774 vs 0.4245) and instruction-following (0.4409 vs 0.4564). So your entire pipeline's advantage over a raw Claude one-shot is being partially given back on presentation. Closing readability to just the claude-3-7 level (+0.047 on that dim, ~ +0.8 raw points) is worth roughly +0.005 overall — small, but it is free and it is the cheapest point on the board.

findings.claim: LLM judges given EXPLICIT criteria (exactly RACE's design) still punish style violations 5-7x harder than factual errors: making a response concise costs a 63% loss rate while giving a WRONG ANSWER costs only 13%.
findings.detail: SOS-Bench / 'Style Outweighs Substance', Feuer et al., ICLR 2025. Verified verbatim from raw HTML: 'Sarcasm and conciseness are heavily penalized, while incorrect and bland responses are only weakly penalized. Intervention Loss | Base 00% | Bland 08% | Wrong 13% | Concise 63% | Sarcastic 96%'. Judge = gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18 on Arena-Hard-Auto with a MODIFIED judge template that supplies explicit criteria. The section heading is literally 'Given explicit judgment criteria, LLM-Judges implicitly favor completeness and style' — i.e. RACE's per-task criteria list does NOT immunize it. They also report judge score being near-perfectly rank-correlated with a style factor (Spearman ~0.999) vs safety (~0.827). CAVEAT for you: the dominant channel here is completeness/verbosity, and you are ALREADY long (7,692 cleaned words vs the reference's ~8,400), so this lever is largely spent. Do NOT read this as license to pad.

findings.claim: In the canonical measured decomposition (LMArena Bradley-Terry style control), LENGTH dominates and markdown structure is explicitly 'second order': controlling for both, length coefficient = 0.249 vs markdown list 0.031, header 0.024, bold 0.019. Since POLARIS is already at human length, the big style lever is unavailable and only the small ones remain.
findings.detail: Four style features enter the BT regression: answer token length, # markdown headers, # markdown bold, # markdown lists. Fitted coefficients — Control Both: length 0.249, list 0.031, header 0.024, bold 0.019. Control Markdown Only (length uncontrolled, so markdown absorbs length's effect): list 0.111, header 0.044, bold 0.056. Control Length Only: length 0.267. Paper's own summary: 'length was the dominant style factor. All other markdown effects are second order.' Rank shifts after control were large (GPT-4o-mini 6->11; Grok-2-mini 6->18; Claude 3 Opus 16->10; Claude 3.5 Sonnet 2->1 on Hard Prompts). Two caveats: (a) this is HUMAN preference, not an LLM judge, though it is the best-powered decomposition that exists; (b) the fact that markdown-only control inflates the list/header/bold coefficients 2-4x shows most of the apparent 'structure bonus' in the wild is really length in disguise. Practical read for POLARIS: adding headers/bullets/tables WITHOUT adding words should be expected to yield a small direct effect. The payoff has to come through the substance channel (forcing synthesis), not the cosmetic one.

findings.claim: Style-only manipulation with zero content change reliably raises LLM-judge scores by +1 to +2 points on a 9-point scale with >65% attack success — which, converted through the RACE formula, is worth +0.04 to +0.08 overall. That is an upper bound on how much of your 0.088 gap could in principle be presentation.
findings.detail: BITE ('Turning Bias into Bugs: Bandit-Guided Style Manipulation Attacks on LLM Judges'). Bandit-selects among 8 semantically-null transforms: markdown (headers/bold/italics/blockquotes), verbosity, JSON structuring, emoji, sentiment, authority, bandwagon, distraction. Reported: +1-2 points average on 9-point pointwise grading across five judges (o3-mini, Gemini-2.5-Flash, Llama-3.3-70B, DeepSeek-R1, Qwen3-235B) on AlpacaEval 2.0 / Arena-Hard-Auto; attack success rate >65%; pairwise normalized deltas 1.347-2.010; and +0.5-1.5 points on 10-point scales in an automated-peer-review setting. Regression: verbosity and italics have 'consistently strong, positive coefficients across all judges'. CONFIDENCE MEDIUM because I read the paper's HTML via a summarizer and did NOT byte-verify these specific figures the way I did for SOS-Bench/WebWeaver/DRB-II; also the effect is adversarially optimized (a bandit searching over transforms), so it is an upper bound, not what an honest reformat would yield. Also note RACE forces a criterion-by-criterion comparative analysis against a strong reference in the same prompt, which empirically suppresses shallow style bias relative to these single-answer settings.

findings.claim: RACE does NOT randomize position: the target report is always article_1 and the human reference is always article_2, in a single merged prompt. There is no swap, no shuffle, and no position-bias correction anywhere in the scoring code.
findings.detail: deepresearch_bench_race.py: user_prompt = merged_score_prompt.format(..., article_1=target_article, article_2=reference_article). Grepped the whole scoring path (deepresearch_bench_race.py, utils/*.py, prompt/score_prompt_en.py) for swap|shuffle|random|position — zero hits in the scoring logic. utils/score_calculator.py reads article_1_score as target and article_2_score as reference unconditionally. Implication: whatever first-position preference your gpt-5.5 evaluator has is a constant that you ALREADY receive; it is not an exploitable lever, but it does mean any position-bias term is baked into every published number on the leaderboard, including ADORE's 0.5265 and your 0.4382. It also means k=5 repeats sample judge noise but not order noise — your pinned k=5 does not average out position effects.

findings.claim: ADORE's reported 0.5265 could not be traced to a paper that explains its mechanism; the '52.65 / comp 52.22 / insight 54.37 / IF 51.11 / read 52.18' figures surface only via leaderboard-style secondary sources. Do NOT plan against an assumed ADORE architecture.
findings.detail: I searched arXiv and the web repeatedly for an ADORE paper. Search engines attributed the numbers to arXiv 2601.18267 ('Orchestrating Specialized Agents for Trustworthy Enterprise RAG'), but when I fetched that PDF its content is about evidence grounding and claim traceability for enterprise RAG, with NO visible RACE breakdown table, NO ablation, and no discussion of report formatting/outlines — I could not confirm it is the ADORE of the leaderboard, and the search engine may have conflated them. Given your explicit warning about stale/plausible numbers (the 48.88 incident), I am flagging this: the 0.5265 target is a leaderboard number, and any story about HOW ADORE gets it is currently unverified. The mechanism-bearing, RACE-native SOTA-class system you CAN read and copy is WebWeaver (50.58, arXiv 2509.13312), whose ablations are reproduced above. Recommend treating WebWeaver's outline+section-by-section+scoped-retrieval writer as the actionable reference implementation and ignoring ADORE's internals until a real paper is found.

findings.claim: Bottom line on the 0.088: roughly 0.018-0.024 (20-27%) is reachable by presentation alone; the remaining ~0.065-0.070 is substance, specifically cross-source synthesis. The highest-leverage move is one that converts a formatting change INTO a substance change.
findings.detail: Synthesis of the above, with the arithmetic in finding #3. The single recommendation the evidence converges on: build the 'summary table of key studies and their findings' that task-72 criterion D1 names verbatim, and write the report section-by-section from an outline with per-section scoped evidence (WebWeaver's +7.3 insight ablation). A comparison table is not cosmetic — you cannot fill a row-per-study, column-per-dimension table without performing exactly the cross-source reconciliation that insight criteria 1, 2 and 4 (0.70 of the insight dimension, 0.224 of the total score) reward, and it structurally breaks the span-grounding constraint that forbids those sentences. Likewise, cutting 633-word paragraphs to ~142 words (reference norm) forces one-idea-per-paragraph (criterion S2) and creates the topic-sentence slots where a cross-source claim must be asserted before it is supported. CONFIDENCE MEDIUM on the exact split because it assumes the judge does not exhibit a large cross-dimension halo; SOS-Bench and BITE suggest some leakage from style into substance ratings exists, which would push the presentation share higher than 27%, while DeepResearch Bench II's 89% presentation ceiling suggests it cannot push it much higher.

findings.claim: PREMISE BUSTED: the RACE reference is NOT human-written. It is Gemini-2.5-pro Deep Research output from April 2025. 'Human reference = 0.5000' is false.
findings.detail: Direct quote from the paper (Experimental Setup): 'The reference reports (Rref) used in RACE's scoring methodology were selected from deep research articles generated by the Gemini-2.5-pro-based Deep Research, as available in April 2025.' Corroborated independently by the leaderboards: gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch scores 49.98 (gpt-5.5 judge) and 49.71 (original judge) — i.e. ~0.50, because it is being scored against a sibling sample of its own output. CONSEQUENCE FOR POLARIS: the thing we must beat is a known, imitable, year-old LLM's house style — not a human. Its 59 paragraphs / 142 words-per-para / 24 H3 / 10 tables / 91 bullets is *Gemini DR's* template, and it has known weaknesses (it is the weakest-insight report among all >50 systems). The '0.5000 by construction' intuition is still right (reference vs itself = 0.5), but the 'human' part is wrong and it has been leading us to over-respect the reference.

findings.claim: PREMISE BUSTED (stale number, again): ADORE at 0.5265 is NOT SOTA. Current SOTA on the gpt-5.5 evaluator we are pinned to is cellcog-max at 0.5578. Our real gap is 0.1196, not 0.088.
findings.detail: I pulled the live leaderboard CSVs directly. The HF Space has TWO leaderboards, and one is literally named for our judge. data_gpt55/leaderboard.csv (gpt-5.5 evaluator, the one POLARIS is pinned to): cellcog-max 55.78, WhaleCloud-DocChain_0612 54.78, bodhi 54.07, lunon 53.51, dalpha 53.10, sourcery 51.17, gemini-2.5-pro-DR 49.98, openai-DR 47.84, perplexity 43.05, grok 41.22. data/leaderboard.csv (original judge): qianfan_deepresearch_0430 58.03, ZTE-Nebula 57.27, Link 57.08, zhipu 57.06 ... tavily-research 52.44. ADORE appears on NEITHER. ADORE=52.65 is real (arXiv 2601.18267, 'Orchestrating Specialized Agents for Trustworthy Enterprise RAG') and the paper does claim 'rank #1 on the leaderboard', but its Table 2 baselines are LangChain(Tavily)/OpenAI-DR — an old snapshot. It is now mid-pack. NOTE: POLARIS 0.4382 currently sits between perplexity (43.05) and openai-DR (47.84) on our judge. WORSE: that same ADORE paper is almost certainly the source of our 'human reference' error — it states 'the benchmark is calibrated such that a score of 50.0 represents the human-written reference', which contradicts the primary DRB paper.

findings.claim: The overall score is a ratio of WEIGHTED TOTALS, not a weighted average of the four published dimension scores. Marginal gain per absolute criterion point is proportional to the dimension weight, making INSIGHT (0.32 on task 72) the single highest-leverage point to win.
findings.detail: From score_calculator.py + deepresearch_bench_race.py. Per dimension d: S_d(R) = (sum_k w_dk * s_dk) / (sum_k w_dk)  [criterion weights, renormalized]. Then S_int(R) = sum_d W_d * S_d(R)  [dimension weights]. Then overall = S_int(tgt) / (S_int(tgt) + S_int(ref)). The four *reported* dimension numbers are computed separately as S_d(tgt)/(S_d(tgt)+S_d(ref)) — so the published overall is NOT the weighted mean of the published dimensions (ratio-of-sums != sum-of-ratios). Differentiating: d(overall)/dS_d(tgt) = W_d * S_int(ref) / (S_int(tgt)+S_int(ref))^2 — i.e. the payoff for +1 absolute point is exactly proportional to W_d, independent of how the reference scores on that dimension. So on task 72 an absolute point of insight (0.32) is worth 2.3x an absolute point of readability (0.14). ARITHMETIC ON OUR POSITION: overall 0.4382 => S_int(tgt)/S_int(ref) = 0.780. To merely TIE the reference (0.50) we need +28% absolute weighted score. To reach cellcog-max (0.5578) we need +62%. This is a much bigger lift than the 0.088 framing implied.

findings.claim: There is NO position randomization: the target report is ALWAYS article_1 and the reference is ALWAYS article_2, on every single judge call.
findings.detail: deepresearch_bench_race.py line ~99: merged_score_prompt.format(task_prompt=..., article_1=target_article, article_2=reference_article, criteria_list=...). grep for shuffle|random|swap|position over the whole file returns nothing. So the judge sees us first and the Gemini reference second, always. Two consequences: (a) whatever positional bias the judge has is a CONSTANT, not noise — it does not average out over k=5 or over tasks, and it is already baked into every leaderboard number, so we should not try to 'correct' for it; (b) the judge is doing an explicitly COMPARATIVE read (the prompt says 'deeply compare two articles', 'Comparative Evaluation', and emits an 'analysis' field contrasting them before scoring). Scores are not independent absolute judgments. What matters is being *visibly, contrastively* better on each named criterion — a report that is good in isolation but offers nothing the reference lacks will land at ~0.5.

findings.claim: The INSIGHT criteria are auto-generated per task with an explicit instruction to reward reasoning ACROSS sources and to punish exactly what POLARIS does (span-grounded listing).
findings.detail: criteria_prompt_en.py, generate_eval_criteria_prompt_insight. Core Requirement #2 verbatim: 'Beyond Surface-Level: Criteria should assess analytical depth, logical rigor, originality of insights, and value of conclusions, NOT JUST INFORMATION LISTING.' The few-shot example criteria it is told to imitate: 'Depth of Analysis of Core Drivers and Mechanisms' — 'deeply analyzes their interplay and causal mechanisms, rather than a superficial listing'; 'Sophistication in Uncovering Market Impacts' — 'goes beyond obvious impacts to uncover subtle or SECOND-ORDER effects'; 'Logical Coherence and Justification of Strategies'; 'Originality and Strategic Value of Insights' — 'challenges conventional wisdom'; 'Future Outlook and Adaptability'. Every one of these is satisfiable ONLY by a sentence whose truth-maker is the relation between multiple sources, not any single span. Our per-sentence span-grounding invariant makes these criteria structurally unscoreable for us — this is the direct mechanical cause of insight being our worst dimension at the heaviest weight. The static-criteria fallback (score_prompt_en.py) names the same four: Analysis Depth and Originality / Logical Reasoning and Causal Relationships / Problem Insight and Solutions / Forward-Looking and Inspirational Thinking.

findings.claim: LENGTH IS NOT THE LEVER. Onyx beat the reference on task 72 (0.5626) with a 4,554-word report — shorter than ours. Its edge is paragraph granularity and structure, not volume.
findings.detail: Onyx published per-task raw RACE results AND its actual reports. Its task-72 numbers (from eval_results/run_1/raw_results.jsonl, id=72): overall 0.5626, comprehensiveness 0.5662, insight 0.5813, instruction_following 0.5599, readability 0.5213 — vs POLARIS 0.4382/0.4549/0.4238/0.4409/0.3774. I measured its report_logs/72.md: ANSWER body = 4,554 words, 11 H3, 25 H4, 79 bullets, 0 tables, 24 body paragraphs averaging 49 WORDS. POLARIS: 7,692 words, 12 paragraphs averaging 633 words, 0 subsections, 0 tables, 0 bullets. Meanwhile the #1 system overall (qianfan, 58.03) went the opposite way on length — 71,504 words on task 72 — yet lands in the same place. The variable the two winners SHARE, and we lack, is not length: it is paragraph granularity (49 and 130 words/para vs our 633) and heading density. Our 633-word monoliths give the judge no addressable unit to map onto any named criterion.

findings.claim: The insight-manufacturing device in the #1 report is a NAMED SECTION per chapter whose entire job is cross-source adjudication — 'Synthesis', 'Sources of Divergence', 'Reconciling', 'Comparative Assessment'.
findings.detail: Qianfan (rank 1, 58.03) published its literal task-72 report. Structure: 11 H1 chapters, 61 H2, 25 H3, 13 tables, 492 paragraphs at ~130 words. Every chapter TERMINATES in an adjudication section: '2.7 Comparative Assessment: Convergences, Divergences, and Implications'; '3.4 Sources of Divergence: Measurement, Horizon, Geography, and Identification'; '3.5 Synthesis: What the Aggregate Evidence Does and Does Not Establish'; '4.6 Methodological Reconciliation: Occupation-Level versus Task-Level Exposure'; '5.1 Reconciling the divergent trajectories'; '8.7 Synthesis: Organizational Implementation as the Decisive Intermediary'. The PROSE inside them explicitly labels epistemic status across the corpus: 'Bringing the four preceding sections together, the aggregate-employment evidence supports a set of carefully calibrated claims while leaving several first-order questions genuinely open. Three propositions enjoy reasonably strong **emerging consensus** across the methodologically diverse literature. First, **meaningful local displacement effects are well-documented**... Second, **offsetting product-demand and complementarity channels operate with quantitatively first-order magnitudes**...' Note the bolded claim-topic sentences. ACTIONABLE FOR POLARIS: this is a structural, not stylistic, fix — allocate explicit sections that are LICENSED to contain inference-over-sources (consensus / divergence / reconciliation / what-the-evidence-does-not-establish) and exempt them from the per-sentence span-grounding invariant, grounding them instead in the SET of spans they adjudicate. Confidence medium because this is observed correlation in the top reports, not an ablation.

findings.claim: Every system that beats the reference is INSIGHT-LED; every system that loses to it is READABILITY-LED. Insight is where the Gemini reference is weakest and headroom is largest; readability is the hardest dimension to beat it on.
findings.detail: Computed insight-minus-readability from the gpt-5.5 leaderboard: cellcog-max +5.14 (57.08 vs 51.94), lunon +4.35, WhaleCloud +2.85, bodhi +2.73, sourcery +2.54, gemini-ref +0.34, openai-DR -0.93, perplexity -4.76, grok -7.60. The sign flips exactly at the 50-line. Same on the original leaderboard, more extremely: qianfan insight 61.48 vs readability 54.34; zhipu 60.14 vs 53.88. And Onyx's 10-run means: insight 0.5683 (highest dim) vs readability 0.5239 (lowest dim). INTERPRETATION: no top system beats the Gemini reference by much on readability — they all top out around 52 — because Gemini DR's formatting is genuinely excellent. Insight is the soft spot: winners clear it by 5-11 points. This says the ceiling on 'make it prettier' is ~+2 over reference, whereas the ceiling on 'make it reason across sources' is ~+11. Combined with the weight (0.32 vs 0.14), insight is where essentially all of the available score is.

findings.claim: POLARIS's readability (0.3774) is below every system on the leaderboard by 8+ points — worse than grok. This is not a polish problem; it is the missing scoring surface.
findings.detail: Worst readability on the gpt-5.5 board is grok-deeper-search at 45.72; every other system is 46-53. POLARIS is 37.74. We are 8 points below the floor of the entire field. The readability criteria (score_prompt_en.py static set, and the task-specific generated equivalents) include 'Clear Structure and Logic' (introduction/body/conclusion, coherent paragraph organization) and 'Information Presentation and Visual Elements' ('effectively uses formatting, headings, lists, emphasis, etc. ... and appropriately uses charts'). With 0 subsections, 0 tables, 0 bullets and 633-word paragraphs we score near-zero on two of four readability criteria. IMPORTANT SECOND-ORDER EFFECT: the same intervention pays into the other dimensions — tables score under comprehensiveness's 'Data and Factual Support', and named synthesis headings create the addressable surface the judge maps insight criteria onto.
